participants.extracted: The study included 101 adults with temporomandibular disorder (TMD) pain, comprising 85 women and 16 men, recruited from university dental clinics, other dental referrers, and community advertisements. Participants had unilateral or bilateral pain in the temporomandibular joint area that had been present on a daily basis for at least 3 months and met Research Diagnostic Criteria for TMD. The mean age was 39.4 years (SD 12.1); most participants were White (79%), with 9% Black, 9% Hispanic, and 3% Other. Forty-one percent were married or cohabiting, and the average education level was 14.7 years (SD 2.5). They had experienced chronic TMD pain for an average of 6.7 years (SD 6.6), with a mean baseline pain intensity of 3.5 on a 0–6 scale (SD 1.3). Of 196 individuals screened, 121 were eligible and 101 were randomized; follow-up data were available for 88% at posttreatment and 73% at 52 weeks.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to one of two 6-week treatments. The Standard Treatment (STD) consisted of placement of a flat-plane occlusal disoccluding splint, a 5-week course of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medication (naproxen sodium 550 mg twice daily, with acetaminophen substituted when needed), and instruction to follow a soft diet and avoid painful or extreme jaw-opening foods. Patients in STD also attended weekly progress-check visits focused on adherence and monitoring, without cognitive-behavioral counseling. The Standard Treatment plus Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment (STD+CBT) group received the same splint, medication, and soft-diet regimen plus weekly brief cognitive-behavioral coping-skills sessions. CBT included relaxation training, stress management, cognitive restructuring, self-efficacy enhancement, masseter EMG biofeedback-assisted relaxation, habit modification to reduce clenching and bruxing, and homework practice of the skills taught in treatment.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure pain at baseline, posttreatment (6 weeks), and every 3 months through 52 weeks using the Multidimensional Pain Inventory (MPI) Characteristic Pain Intensity score, calculated on a 0–6 scale from ratings of current pain, average pain, and worst pain in the past week. It also planned to measure pain-related interference with activities at the same time points using the MPI Interference subscale, a 13-item scale scored from 0 to 6 and averaged across items. Depressive symptoms were assessed at the same time points using the 20-item Center for Epidemiological Studies Depression Scale (CES-D).
